title: Sgraffito on air-dry clay
subtitle: Scratching through a slip coat to reveal contrasting clay underneath
excerpt: Sgraffito is a surface decoration technique in which a layer of slip in a contrasting colour is applied to leather-hard clay, then scratched through to reveal the clay body beneath. The scratch lines show as the clay colour against the slip background. Air-dry clay is well suited to the technique at the leather-hard stage. No kiln or wheel is needed.
sourceNotes: Sgraffito technique. Standard surface decoration method in pottery. Encyclopaedia Britannica, 11th edition (1911), ceramics entry. Technique derived from first principles.

content.text: requires two contrasting layers: a clay body and a

coat applied over it. The design is drawn through the wet slip with a sgraffito tool, exposing the clay body colour in the scratched lines. On air-dry clay, a dark slip applied to a pale clay body gives maximum contrast. Acrylic paint diluted 1:3 with water is a practical substitute for coloured clay slip and gives a wide colour range.

attrs.body: Sgraffito works at a specific window: after the slip has dried enough to be matte (not shiny) but before the clay reaches bone-dry. At this point the tool cuts cleanly without dragging the slip or crumbling the clay edge. Too wet: the line smears. Too dry: the slip chips and the edges crumble.
attrs.title: Timing matters

items.name: Coloured slip or acrylic paint diluted 1:3

content.text: before applying the slip. The surface should be firm, not tacky, and should not deform when pressed gently. If the piece is too wet, allow it to air-dry a further hour before proceeding.

Brush two coats of coloured slip over the area to be decorated, allowing the first coat to become matte before applying the second. Apply in smooth strokes in the same direction to minimise brush marks. The slip should be an even, opaque layer. Allow this to dry until the surface is matte but the underlying clay is still slightly damp, typically 20 to 40 minutes.

Use the sgraffito tool to draw lines through the slip coat. Drag the tool in a single fluid stroke, removing the slip cleanly to reveal the clay colour below. The angle of the tool affects the line: held nearly vertical gives a fine clean line; held at a shallow angle gives a broader, softer line. For curved lines, rotate the piece rather than the wrist.
Step 4: Remove slip dust and dry

Scratch lines accumulate small rolls of slip at their edges. Brush these away with a dry soft brush after the slip is fully matte. Do not use a wet sponge at this point, it will drag slip into the scratch lines and obscure them. Allow to dry to bone-dry, then seal with two coats of PVA diluted 1:1.
Simple botanical line drawings, a single stem with leaves, suit the technique well because the lines can be drawn in a single continuous stroke.
Cross-hatching: parallel lines in two directions create a filled area that reads as a mid-tone between the slip and clay colours.

attrs.intro: Problems in sgraffito on air-dry clay.

items.fix: Work within the matte-but-damp window. If the slip has dried completely, lightly mist it with water and wait two minutes before continuing.
items.cause: Slip or clay was too dry when scratched.
items.symptom: Scratch lines are ragged with crumbling edges

items.fix: Wait until the slip surface is matte with no shiny areas. Touch a non-decorated area with a knuckle, it should feel cool but not wet.
items.cause: Slip was still wet when scratching began.
items.symptom: Slip drags into scratch lines and fills them

attrs.heading: Common problems
